news_summary: Innovative sustainable practices in grilling, rapid conversion of coffee waste into clean fuel, successful wildlife conservation efforts, and climate-resilient urban design are advancing environmental protection and adaptation.
news_summary_fr: Des pratiques innovantes et durables en matière de cuisson au barbecue, la transformation rapide des déchets de café en combustible propre, des initiatives fructueuses de conservation de la faune sauvage et un aménagement urbain résilient face au changement climatique contribuent à faire progresser la protection de l'environnement et l'adaptation au changement climatique.
news_summary_es: Las prácticas innovadoras y sostenibles en la preparación de parrilladas, la conversión rápida de los residuos del café en combustible limpio, las iniciativas exitosas de conservación de la fauna silvestre y el diseño urbano resistente al cambio climático están impulsando la protección del medio ambiente y la adaptación al mismo.

articles.title: Choose the Right Type of Grill for Your Sustainable Summer
articles.summary:
The biggest climate decision at your next cookout is not the grill you buy. It...
The post Choose the Right Type of Grill for Your Sustainable Summer appeared first on Earth911.
articles.content:
The biggest climate decision at your next cookout is not the grill you buy. It is what you put on the grates. A backyard charcoal barbecue for four people can release as much greenhouse gas as an 80-mile car drive; and most of that comes from the meat, not the fuel, according to researchers at the University of Sheffield’s Institute for Sustainable Food.

Grilling season is back, and a new grill is a chance to cut the emissions from summer entertaining. But the fuels are only part of the story, and the rankings have shifted since this guide first ran. The U.S. power grid has gotten markedly cleaner, which changes the case for electric grills. Before you head to the store, it helps to understand the carbon impact of charcoal, wood pellets, propane, natural gas, electricity, and solar — and where each one actually lands. And if you’re replacing an old grill, don’t forget to recycle the one you’re retiring.

Start With What’s on the Grates

Cook from scratch and you’re already ahead. Preparing a meal from whole ingredients rather than processed food can lower its global warming and human toxicity potential by up to 35%, according to a study in the Journal of Cleaner Production. That doesn’t make a barbecue carbon-free, though — and the single largest lever you control is protein.

The Sheffield team compared three grilled meals and found the gap is dramatic. A beef-heavy cookout was the most carbon-intensive option they measured, while swapping beef burgers for chicken cut the barbecue’s emissions by roughly a third, and a fully plant-based spread cut them by more than half. The lifecycle impact of the food, especially red meat, typically dwarfs the footprint of the fuel used to cook it. A burger’s beef carries far more embodied carbon than the propane needed to char it.

Put plainly: grilling more vegetables, chicken, or plant-based options is the highest-impact change most people can make at a cookout. The fuel decisions below matter — they’re just the second-biggest lever, not the first.

The Burning Truth About Fuels

Grilling is a science, as any grill master knows. A meal for four takes roughly 30 minutes to an hour on the grates, and different fuels need different lengths of time to reach cooking temperature. The estimates below assume about one hour of cooking to make dinner for four — your actual results will vary with the grill and fuel you use.

Charcoal: The Most Polluting Choice

Dinner for four needs about 300 square inches of grill space for meat, vegetables, and warming buns. Charcoal runs about 30 briquettes per 100 square inches, so you’ll need roughly 90 pieces. At an average briquette weight of about 0.8 ounces, that’s about 4.7 pounds of charcoal for the meal.

Charcoal carries two carbon costs: the embodied carbon of making the briquettes and the emissions from burning them. Manufacturing is the hidden problem. Charcoal is produced by heating wood in a low-oxygen kiln until moisture and volatile compounds cook off, leaving nearly pure carbon, and only about 20% to 35% of the original wood’s energy survives the process. The rest burns off during production. Estimates put the manufacturing footprint at roughly 3.5 to 7 pounds of CO2 per pound of charcoal, before you ever light the grill.

Burning the 90 briquettes for an hour adds about 21 pounds of CO2, plus roughly 0.79 pounds of carbon monoxide and 1.6 pounds of particulate matter. All told, a single charcoal cookout for four exceeds 28 pounds of CO2 along with other pollutants and is by far the most polluting way to grill. Eric Johnson’s lifecycle comparison in Environmental Impact Assessment Review reached a similar verdict: charcoal’s carbon footprint is roughly three times that of liquefied petroleum gas.

There’s a footprint upstream of your patio, too. Worldwide, woodfuel harvest, which includes wood used to make charcoal, accounts for about 30% of the estimated 2.1 billion tons of CO2 released annually by forest degradation across developing countries, according to research published in Carbon Balance and Management. In parts of Africa, charcoal production now rivals outright deforestation as a driver of forest loss. The wood in a U.S. bag of briquettes may be sourced differently, but the global picture is a reminder that “natural” charcoal is not the same as low-impact.

Wood Pellets: Second Place, by a Wide Margin

Wood pellets are a popular charcoal alternative, and some grillers treat them as a low-carbon, renewable fuel. Cooking on a 300-square-inch grill takes about two pounds of pellets.

Embodied carbon varies widely with the wood used, the manufacturing process, and how far the pellets travel. In general, a pound of pellets carries about 0.09 pounds of embodied carbon and produces about 1.8 pounds of CO2 when burned — putting our four-person dinner at roughly 3.78 pounds total. Because pellets burn cleaner than charcoal, the meal generates only a few grams of carbon monoxide, particulate matter, and nitrogen and sulfur compounds. Pellets are the second-worst option on this list, but at a fraction of charcoal’s impact.

The Lower Footprint of Cooking With Gas

Propane and natural gas grills carry a lower environmental impact than charcoal and pellets, mostly because they heat fast and switch off the moment you’re done. That control over cooking time makes a real difference — charcoal keeps throwing off heat and emissions long before the grates are ready and well after the food comes off. Gas also keeps far more of its energy: propane retains roughly 90% of its energy from wellhead to grill, against charcoal’s 20% to 35%.

A propane grill uses about a half pound of propane for the 30 minutes our four-person meal needs. Including the embodied carbon of producing and delivering the fuel, that session totals about 1.99 pounds of CO2.

Natural gas usually requires a gas line plumbed to the home, so propane is more convenient for many grillers. A 30-minute cook uses roughly 1.2 pounds of natural gas, for a footprint of about 1.73 pounds of CO2. One caveat worth naming: natural gas systems can leak methane, a greenhouse gas far more potent than CO2 over the short term, which the combustion math above doesn’t capture.

Electric Grilling Has Quietly Become a Top Choice

For years the conventional wisdom held that electric grills were the dirtiest option, citing a 2003 Oak Ridge National Laboratory analysis from an era when coal dominated the power grid. That conclusion no longer holds, because the grid underneath it has changed.

In 2023, generating a kilowatt-hour of U.S. electricity produced about 0.81 pounds of CO2 on average, according to the U.S. Energy Information Administration — down sharply from the coal-heavy mix of two decades ago, and still falling. The EPA’s emissions database shows grid carbon intensity dropping again by an average of about 3% from 2023 to 2024 as wind, solar, and natural gas displace coal.

Run the math on a modern electric grill, which draws roughly 1.5 to 1.8 kilowatts, and an hour of cooking on the average U.S. grid now produces somewhere in the neighborhood of 1.2 to 1.5 pounds of CO2. That puts it below propane on the typical grid — and well below charcoal — with one feature no fuel-burning grill offers: it gets cleaner every year on its own, as the grid decarbonizes, the same logic that drives heat pumps and electric vehicles. If your power comes from a cleaner-than-average source — rooftop solar, a green-power plan, or a state rich in hydro, wind, or nuclear — the number drops further still. Pair an electric grill with a solar generator and you approach a genuinely zero-carbon cookout.

The honest tradeoff is flavor and feel. Electric grills heat with coils or elements rather than open flame, so they don’t impart the same smoke, and purists notice. Cast-iron grates can deliver sear marks, and add-on smoking accessories help, but this is the option where the cooking experience differs most from a traditional grill.

Solar Cooking: Zero Fuel, Real Limits

Solar ovens use reflectors to concentrate sunlight on a sealed cooking chamber, making a meal with no fuel and no direct emissions. They excel at slow, moist, flavorful cooking — think baking and braising more than searing.

The limits are practical. It takes a genuinely sunny day to reach broiling or grilling temperatures, output drops with the light, and solar cookers don’t produce the signature grill lines many cooks prize. As a complement to another method — or for the right dish on the right afternoon — solar is unbeatable on carbon. As an everyday grill replacement for most households, it’s a harder sell than a clean-grid electric model.

The Carbon Impact of Grilling

A season of cooking 45 meals using each of the options we’ve reviewed will produce the following emissions:

Charcoal: 1,260 lbs. of CO2, plus other pollutants

1,260 lbs. of CO2, plus other pollutants Wood Pellets: 170 lbs. of CO2, plus some other pollutants

170 lbs. of CO2, plus some other pollutants Propane: 90 lbs. of CO2

90 lbs. of CO2 Natural Gas: 78 lbs. of CO2

78 lbs. of CO2 Solar: No CO2

Stretch these single-meal figures across a season of 45 cookouts and the differing environmental become starkly evident. The table below assumes the same four-person meal each time, cooked on the average U.S. grid for the electric estimate.

The Carbon Cost of Grilling, by Fuel Fuel Per Meal (4 people) 45-Meal Season Charcoal 28+ lbs. CO 2 , plus pollutants ~1,260 lbs. CO 2 , plus pollutants Wood pellets ~3.78 lbs. CO 2 ~170 lbs. CO 2 Propane ~1.99 lbs. CO 2 ~90 lbs. CO 2 Natural gas ~1.73 lbs. CO 2 ~78 lbs. CO 2 Electric (avg. U.S. grid) ~1.2–1.5 lbs. CO 2 , falling yearly ~55–65 lbs. CO 2 , falling yearly Electric (clean power) / Solar Near zero Near zero Charcoal, gas, and electric are not roughly equivalent; they’re separated by a factor of three or more. Electric figures assume the average U.S. grid (~0.81 lb CO 2 /kWh, 2023) and drop further on cleaner power. Sources: U.S. EIA; EPA eGRID; University of Sheffield Institute for Sustainable Food.

What You Can Do

If you’re buying a new grill

Consider electric first. On today’s grid it beats propane, and it keeps improving as power generation cleans up, with no equipment swap required.

If you want flame, choose gas over charcoal. Propane and natural gas heat fast and shut off instantly, cutting both fuel use and emissions.

Recycle the grill you’re replacing rather than sending it to landfill. Check Earth911’s recycling locator for locations near you that accept grills, metal, or appliances.

If you’re keeping the grill you have

Cook with the lid down to trap heat and burn less fuel per meal.

Preheat for about 10 minutes instead of 20, and turn a gas grill off the moment the food is done.

If you grill on charcoal, skip lighter fluid and use a chimney starter — a metal cylinder lit with a sheet of newspaper, usually under $20. Choose lump charcoal over briquettes, ideally from a brand that names its wood source, and light only as much as you need.

The change that beats all of the above

Grill more vegetables, chicken, and plant-based options. Shifting even a few beef meals to chicken or veggies each season cuts more emissions than switching fuels does. The food is the bigger lever.

Cook from scratch with whole ingredients to trim the lifecycle footprint of the meal itself.

The grilling choices you make this summer carry a long-term climate cost, or savings. Pick the cleaner fuel where you can, rethink what goes on the grates, and the backyard barbecue stays exactly as good while asking a lot less of the planet.

Related Reading

Editor’s Note: Originally published on May 31, 2023, this article was substantially updated in June 2026.
articles.reasoning: The article provides detailed, evidence-based guidance on reducing greenhouse gas emissions from grilling by choosing cleaner fuels and shifting to plant-based foods, highlighting significant climate benefits accessible to the general public. It offers practical, actionable advice with scientific context, emphasizing measurable environmental impact and encouraging sustainable behavior.
articles.personality_title: How to Grill This Summer with Less Impact on the Planet
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Summer grilling is a popular activity, but it can produce a lot of greenhouse gases that harm the environment. The type of food you cook and the fuel you use for grilling both affect how much pollution your cookout creates.

**What happened** – Researchers at the University of Sheffield studied the carbon footprint of different grilling fuels and foods. They found that the biggest impact comes from the food, especially beef. Charcoal grills produce the most pollution, while electric grills powered by today's cleaner electricity grids create much less. Gas grills (propane or natural gas) are better than charcoal but still more polluting than electric grills. Cooking more vegetables, chicken, or plant-based meals reduces emissions significantly.

**Impact** – This research shows that making smart choices about what you grill and how you grill can lower your carbon footprint. For example, switching from beef burgers to chicken can cut emissions by a third, and choosing an electric grill over charcoal can reduce fuel-related pollution by a large margin. Using solar ovens produces no emissions but works best on sunny days and for slow cooking.

**What's next step** – People can start by choosing electric or gas grills instead of charcoal, recycling old grills, and cooking more plant-based meals. As the electric grid gets cleaner, electric grilling will become even better for the environment. Solar cooking could become a popular option for certain dishes and sunny days.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Choosing cleaner fuels and cooking more plant-based foods can make your summer cookouts much better for the planet without losing the fun of grilling.
articles.personality_title_fr: Comment griller cet été avec moins d’impact sur la planète
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Les barbecues d'été sont très populaires, mais ils peuvent produire beaucoup de gaz à effet de serre qui abîment l'environnement. Le type d'aliments que vous cuisinez et le combustible utilisé influencent la pollution créée.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Des chercheurs de l’Université de Sheffield ont étudié l’empreinte carbone de différents combustibles et aliments pour le barbecue. Ils ont découvert que le plus gros impact vient de la nourriture, surtout le bœuf. Les barbecues au charbon de bois polluent le plus, tandis que les barbecues électriques alimentés par des réseaux électriques plus propres aujourd’hui polluent beaucoup moins. Les barbecues au gaz (propane ou gaz naturel) sont mieux que le charbon, mais plus polluants que les électriques. Manger plus de légumes, de poulet ou de plats végétaux réduit fortement les émissions.

**Impact** – Cette étude montre que faire de bons choix sur ce que vous grillez et comment vous grillez peut réduire votre empreinte carbone. Par exemple, remplacer les burgers de bœuf par du poulet peut réduire les émissions d’un tiers, et choisir un barbecue électrique plutôt que du charbon diminue fortement la pollution liée au combustible. Les fours solaires ne produisent aucune émission, mais fonctionnent mieux par temps ensoleillé et pour une cuisson lente.

**Prochaines étapes** – Les gens peuvent commencer par choisir des barbecues électriques ou au gaz au lieu du charbon, recycler les anciens barbecues et cuisiner plus de plats végétaux. Au fur et à mesure que le réseau électrique se nettoie, le barbecue électrique sera encore meilleur pour l’environnement. La cuisson solaire pourrait devenir une option populaire pour certains plats et jours ensoleillés.

**Phrase clé** – Choisir des combustibles plus propres et cuisiner plus de plats végétaux peut rendre vos barbecues d’été bien meilleurs pour la planète sans perdre le plaisir de griller.
articles.personality_title_es: Cómo hacer barbacoas este verano con menos impacto en el planeta
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Las barbacoas de verano son muy populares, pero pueden producir muchos gases de efecto invernadero que dañan el medio ambiente. El tipo de comida que cocines y el combustible que uses para la barbacoa afectan la cantidad de contaminación.

**Qué pasó** – Investigadores de la Universidad de Sheffield estudiaron la huella de carbono de diferentes combustibles y alimentos para la barbacoa. Encontraron que el mayor impacto viene de la comida, especialmente la carne de res. Las barbacoas de carbón producen más contaminación, mientras que las eléctricas, con la electricidad más limpia de hoy, generan mucho menos. Las de gas (propano o gas natural) son mejores que el carbón, pero más contaminantes que las eléctricas. Cocinar más vegetales, pollo o comidas a base de plantas reduce mucho las emisiones.

**Impacto** – Esta investigación muestra que elegir bien qué y cómo cocinar puede bajar tu huella de carbono. Por ejemplo, cambiar hamburguesas de res por pollo puede reducir las emisiones en un tercio, y usar una barbacoa eléctrica en lugar de carbón disminuye mucho la contaminación por combustible. Los hornos solares no producen emisiones, pero funcionan mejor en días soleados y para cocinar lentamente.

**Próximo paso** – La gente puede empezar eligiendo barbacoas eléctricas o de gas en lugar de carbón, reciclar las viejas y cocinar más comidas a base de plantas. A medida que la red eléctrica se limpia, las barbacoas eléctricas serán aún mejores para el ambiente. La cocina solar podría ser una opción popular para ciertos platos y días soleados.

**Conclusión en una frase** – Elegir combustibles más limpios y cocinar más alimentos vegetales puede hacer que tus barbacoas de verano sean mucho mejores para el planeta sin perder la diversión de cocinar al aire libre.
articles.image_prompt: A warm, detailed painting of a backyard grill scene featuring three stylized grills side-by-side: a glowing charcoal grill emitting subtle smoky spirals shaped like heavy carbon molecules; a sleek electric grill with soft blue and green light beams symbolizing clean energy; and a vibrant platter overflowing with colorful vegetables, plant-based foods, and chicken silhouettes arranged on natural wooden grates, all set against a simple, earthy background of soft browns and greens to evoke sustainable summer cooking and mindful choices.

articles.title: Wet coffee waste becomes coal-grade fuel in under two minutes
articles.summary:
BY THE OPTIMIST DAILY EDITORIAL TEAM Researchers at the Korea Institute of Geoscience and Mineral Resources developed a plasma-based process that converts wet coffee grounds into coal-grade biochar in under 90 seconds, with no pre-drying required. The system, described in Chemical Engineering Journal, uses high-temperature plasma flames to process moisture-rich biomass directly, with the water content […]
The post Wet coffee waste becomes coal-grade fuel in under two minutes first appeared on The Optimist Daily: Making Solutions the News.
articles.content:
BY THE OPTIMIST DAILY EDITORIAL TEAM

Researchers at the Korea Institute of Geoscience and Mineral Resources developed a plasma-based process that converts wet coffee grounds into coal-grade biochar in under 90 seconds, with no pre-drying required. The system, described in Chemical Engineering Journal, uses high-temperature plasma flames to process moisture-rich biomass directly, with the water content working as part of the conversion rather than against it.

Why wet biomass has been a persistent problem

High moisture content has long been one of the hardest obstacles in biomass energy recovery. Most conversion technologies require feedstocks to be dried before processing, which adds cost, energy, and time to a supply chain that is already difficult to operate at scale. Spent coffee grounds are particularly moisture-heavy, and conventional approaches treat that water as a problem to be removed before anything useful can happen.

The KIGAM team’s Flame Plasma Pyrolysis system takes a different approach. Plasma flames generated from liquefied petroleum gas and compressed air reach temperatures between 1,470 and 1,650 degrees Fahrenheit (800 to 900 degrees Celsius). At those temperatures, water trapped inside coffee particles vaporizes rapidly, building internal pressure until the particles fracture. Researchers call this the “popcorn effect”: the steam-driven bursts open the biomass structure, increase porosity, and accelerate carbonization faster than dry processing allows. Under optimized conditions, complete conversion takes 90 seconds.

What the resulting material delivers

The biochar is competitive with anthracite coal in energy content. Its heating value of 29.0 megajoules per kilogram is about 33 percent higher than that of untreated coffee grounds, and fixed carbon content rose from 15.6 percent to 46.2 percent. Sulfur compounds were fully eliminated, removing a combustion emissions concern that conventional coal does not clear. The material’s specific surface area also increased substantially, producing a porous carbon product with uses beyond fuel: activated carbon production, filtration, and industrial adsorption.

How it compares to other biomass processing methods

Speed is where Flame Plasma Pyrolysis separates itself most clearly. Hydrothermal carbonization, one of the more established approaches for wet biomass, typically requires one to six hours; torrefaction runs 30 minutes or more. The KIGAM system completes the same conversion in 90 seconds, using combustion-generated plasma rather than electricity-intensive electrode-based devices, which keeps energy costs lower. The process also produced minimal smoke and tar compared to conventional biomass treatment, reducing downstream handling considerably.

Beyond coffee grounds

Although the study focused on spent coffee grounds, lead researcher Dr. Taejun Park described the potential as considerably broader. Food waste, sewage sludge, and agricultural residues all share the high-moisture characteristics that have historically made biomass recovery expensive and difficult, and the team believes the same approach applies. Park put it plainly: wet organic waste is a feedstock, not a disposal problem. Commercial-scale testing with additional waste types is the next step.

Source study: Chemical Engineering Journal— Rapid conversion of wet spent coffee grounds into high-calorific biochar via drying-free flame plasma pyrolysis for process intensification

Did this solution stand out? Share it with a friend or support our mission by becoming an Emissary.
articles.source: The Optimist Daily: Making Solutions the News
articles.reasoning: The article reports a significant technological breakthrough in converting wet biomass waste, specifically coffee grounds, into a high-energy biochar fuel rapidly and efficiently without pre-drying. This innovation addresses a major challenge in biomass energy recovery, has broad potential applications for various types of wet organic waste, and offers environmental benefits by providing a cleaner alternative to coal and reducing waste disposal issues.
articles.personality_title: New plasma method turns wet coffee waste into fuel in 90 seconds
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Biomass energy uses organic waste like plants or food scraps to make fuel. But wet materials, such as used coffee grounds, are hard to turn into fuel because the water inside them must usually be dried first, which takes a lot of time and energy.

**What happened** – Researchers in South Korea created a new process using very hot plasma flames that can turn wet coffee grounds directly into a type of charcoal fuel called biochar in just 90 seconds. This method heats the wet coffee so fast that the water inside causes the material to pop and break apart, speeding up the fuel-making process without needing to dry it first.

**Impact** – This biochar has energy similar to coal but burns cleaner because it has no sulfur. The process is much faster than older methods, which can take from 30 minutes to several hours. It also produces less smoke and tar, making it easier to handle and better for the environment. This could help turn lots of wet organic waste into useful fuel.

**What’s next step** – The researchers want to test this plasma method on other wet wastes like food scraps and sewage sludge. If it works, it could become a new way to recycle many types of wet waste into clean energy on a larger scale.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Scientists have developed a fast and clean plasma technique that converts wet coffee waste into coal-like fuel in under two minutes without drying.
articles.personality_title_fr: Une nouvelle méthode plasma transforme les déchets de café humide en combustible en 90 secondes
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – L'énergie de la biomasse utilise des déchets organiques comme les plantes ou les restes alimentaires pour produire du combustible. Mais les matériaux humides, comme le marc de café utilisé, sont difficiles à convertir car il faut généralement les sécher d'abord, ce qui prend beaucoup de temps et d'énergie.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Des chercheurs en Corée du Sud ont créé un nouveau procédé utilisant des flammes de plasma très chaudes qui transforment directement le marc de café humide en biocharbon, un type de charbon de bois combustible, en seulement 90 secondes. La chaleur rapide fait que l’eau à l’intérieur fait éclater la matière, accélérant la transformation sans avoir besoin de séchage préalable.

**Impact** – Ce biochar a une énergie similaire au charbon mais brûle plus proprement car il ne contient pas de soufre. Le procédé est beaucoup plus rapide que les méthodes classiques, qui prennent de 30 minutes à plusieurs heures. Il produit aussi moins de fumée et de goudron, ce qui facilite la manipulation et est meilleur pour l’environnement. Cela pourrait aider à transformer beaucoup de déchets organiques humides en énergie utile.

**Prochaine étape** – Les chercheurs veulent tester cette méthode plasma sur d’autres déchets humides comme les restes alimentaires et les boues d’épuration. Si cela fonctionne, cela pourrait devenir une nouvelle façon de recycler de nombreux déchets humides en énergie propre à grande échelle.

**Résumé en une phrase** – Des scientifiques ont développé une technique plasma rapide et propre qui convertit les déchets de café humide en combustible proche du charbon en moins de deux minutes sans séchage.
articles.personality_title_es: Nuevo método de plasma convierte residuos húmedos de café en combustible en 90 segundos
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – La energía de biomasa utiliza residuos orgánicos como plantas o restos de comida para crear combustible. Pero los materiales húmedos, como los posos de café usados, son difíciles de convertir porque normalmente deben secarse primero, lo que consume mucho tiempo y energía.

**Qué pasó** – Investigadores en Corea del Sur desarrollaron un proceso que usa llamas de plasma muy calientes para convertir directamente posos de café húmedos en biochar, un tipo de carbón para combustible, en solo 90 segundos. El calor rápido hace que el agua dentro provoque que el material estalle, acelerando la conversión sin necesidad de secado previo.

**Impacto** – Este biochar tiene una energía similar al carbón pero arde más limpio porque no contiene azufre. El proceso es mucho más rápido que los métodos antiguos, que tardan entre 30 minutos y varias horas. También produce menos humo y alquitrán, facilitando su manejo y siendo mejor para el medio ambiente. Esto podría ayudar a convertir muchos residuos orgánicos húmedos en energía útil.

**Próximo paso** – Los investigadores quieren probar este método de plasma con otros residuos húmedos como restos de comida y lodos de alcantarillado. Si funciona, podría convertirse en una nueva forma de reciclar muchos tipos de residuos húmedos en energía limpia a gran escala.

**Frase clave** – Científicos han desarrollado una técnica rápida y limpia con plasma que convierte residuos húmedos de café en combustible similar al carbón en menos de dos minutos sin secado.
articles.image_prompt: A warm, detailed painting of glowing plasma flames gently enveloping steaming, cracked coffee grounds that burst open like popcorn, transforming into rich, porous black biochar pieces, set against a simple, soft background of earthy browns and muted reds symbolizing rapid, clean energy conversion.

articles.title: Cambridge college swift boxes used for first time
articles.summary: The endangered birds are nesting for the first time at the college, which delights its master.
articles.content:
The first swifts have nested in boxes installed at a University of Cambridge college which is hoping to help stem the decline of the birds and a lack of natural nesting sites.

Swifts are endangered, external and were added to the Red list of Birds of Conservation Concern in the UK in 2021, with numbers plummeting by 70% between 1995 and 2024, the RSPB said.

While there are swift boxes at a number of university sites in the city, boxes were only added to Darwin College three years ago.

Its master, Mike Rands, said: "We have actually got birds nesting in one of our boxes for the first time this year so we will watch this space with great excitement."

Rands has been at the heart of the university's efforts to encourage swift colonies in the city.

He is also the founding director of the University of Cambridge's Cambridge Conservation Initiative at the David Attenborough Building, and former chief executive of BirdLife International.
articles.reasoning: The article reports a positive conservation milestone where endangered swifts have successfully nested in artificial boxes at a Cambridge college, contributing to efforts to stem their population decline. This has broad environmental significance as it supports biodiversity and species recovery, with clear context and details about the conservation initiative and its impact.
articles.personality_title: Endangered swifts nest for first time in Cambridge college boxes
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Swifts are small birds that are endangered in the UK. Their numbers have dropped by 70% since 1995 because they are losing natural places to build nests.

**What happened** – At Darwin College, part of the University of Cambridge, special boxes were put up three years ago to help swifts find new homes. This year, for the first time, swifts have successfully nested in one of these boxes.

**Impact** – This is important because it shows the boxes can help save swifts by giving them safe places to live. It is a small but hopeful step in protecting a bird species that is disappearing quickly.

**What's next step** – The college and conservation groups will continue to watch the swifts and may add more boxes. They hope this will encourage more swifts to nest and help their population grow.

**One-sentence takeaway** – For the first time, endangered swifts have used special nest boxes at a Cambridge college, showing a promising way to protect these birds.
articles.personality_title_fr: Des martinets en danger nichent pour la première fois dans des boîtes à Cambridge
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Les martinets sont de petits oiseaux en danger au Royaume-Uni. Leur nombre a chuté de 70 % depuis 1995 car ils perdent leurs lieux naturels de nidification.

**Ce qui s'est passé** – Au Darwin College de l'Université de Cambridge, des boîtes spéciales ont été installées il y a trois ans pour aider les martinets à trouver un nouveau refuge. Cette année, pour la première fois, des martinets ont réussi à nicher dans l'une de ces boîtes.

**Impact** – C’est important car cela montre que ces boîtes peuvent aider à sauver les martinets en leur offrant un endroit sûr pour vivre. C’est une petite avancée pleine d’espoir pour protéger une espèce qui disparaît rapidement.

**Prochaine étape** – Le collège et les groupes de conservation vont continuer à surveiller les martinets et pourraient installer plus de boîtes. Ils espèrent que cela encouragera plus de martinets à nicher et aidera leur population à grandir.

**Résumé en une phrase** – Pour la première fois, des martinets en danger ont utilisé des boîtes à nid spéciales à Cambridge, montrant une solution prometteuse pour protéger ces oiseaux.
articles.personality_title_es: Los vencejos en peligro anidan por primera vez en cajas de un colegio de Cambridge
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Los vencejos son pequeños pájaros en peligro en el Reino Unido. Su número ha caído un 70 % desde 1995 porque están perdiendo sus lugares naturales para anidar.

**Qué pasó** – En el Darwin College de la Universidad de Cambridge, hace tres años se colocaron cajas especiales para ayudar a los vencejos a encontrar nuevos hogares. Este año, por primera vez, los vencejos han anidado con éxito en una de estas cajas.

**Impacto** – Esto es importante porque muestra que las cajas pueden ayudar a salvar a los vencejos dándoles lugares seguros para vivir. Es un pequeño paso esperanzador para proteger a una especie que está desapareciendo rápidamente.

**Próximo paso** – El colegio y los grupos de conservación seguirán observando a los vencejos y podrían poner más cajas. Esperan que esto anime a más vencejos a anidar y ayude a que su población crezca.

**Resumen en una frase** – Por primera vez, vencejos en peligro han usado cajas de anidación especiales en un colegio de Cambridge, mostrando una forma prometedora de proteger a estas aves.
articles.image_prompt: A warm, detailed painting of a cluster of simple wooden swift nesting boxes mounted on the brick walls of a historic Cambridge college building, with small, sleek swifts entering and leaving the boxes at dusk, their graceful silhouettes contrasted against a soft, natural palette of muted earth tones and gentle twilight sky.

articles.title: 'Architecture, city design a frontline of defense' against heatwaves, expert says
articles.summary: FRANCE 24's Sharon Gaffney discusses cooling solutions in urban environments with Ronita Bardhan, Professor of Sustainable Built Environment and Health at the University of Cambridge. She says that "ultimately architecture and city design is becoming more of a public health infrastructure frontline of defense in this warming world". The record-breaking heatwave in Europe has caused more than 1,300 excess deaths, according to the WHO.
articles.content:
FRANCE 24's Sharon Gaffney discusses cooling solutions in urban environments with Ronita Bardhan, Professor of Sustainable Built Environment and Health at the University of Cambridge. She says that "ultimately architecture and city design is becoming more of a public health infrastructure frontline of defense in this warming world". The record-breaking heatwave in Europe has caused more than 1,300 excess deaths, according to the WHO.

Video by: Sharon GAFFNEY
articles.reasoning: The article highlights the role of architecture and urban design as a frontline defense against heatwaves, which is a significant and actionable approach to mitigating the impacts of climate change on public health. It addresses a broad societal issue with tangible benefits for urban populations facing increasing heat risks.
articles.personality_title: How city design helps protect people from deadly heatwaves
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Europe recently faced a record-breaking heatwave that caused over 1,300 extra deaths, according to the World Health Organization (WHO). Hot weather like this can be dangerous, especially in cities where buildings and roads absorb and hold heat.

**What happened** – Ronita Bardhan, a professor at the University of Cambridge, explained in an interview that the way cities and buildings are designed plays a key role in protecting people from heatwaves. She said architecture and city planning are becoming important tools to keep people safe and healthy as the planet gets warmer.

**Impact** – This idea is important because it shows that cooling cities isn’t just about using air conditioners or fans. Changing how buildings are made and how cities are planned can help reduce heat naturally. For example, adding more trees, using materials that reflect sunlight, and designing streets to allow air flow can all cool down urban areas and lower health risks.

**What's next step** – Experts and city planners will likely work more on creating cooler urban spaces by using smart designs and materials. This approach could become a common part of building new neighborhoods or improving existing ones to help protect people from future heatwaves.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Designing cities and buildings thoughtfully is becoming a key way to protect people from dangerous heat as climate change causes more extreme temperatures.
articles.personality_title_fr: Comment la conception des villes protège les habitants des vagues de chaleur mortelles
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – L'Europe a récemment connu une vague de chaleur record qui a causé plus de 1 300 décès supplémentaires, selon l'Organisation mondiale de la santé (OMS). La chaleur peut être dangereuse, surtout en ville où les bâtiments et les routes retiennent la chaleur.

**Ce qui s'est passé** – Ronita Bardhan, professeure à l'Université de Cambridge, a expliqué dans une interview que la façon dont les villes et les bâtiments sont conçus joue un rôle clé pour protéger les gens des vagues de chaleur. Elle a déclaré que l'architecture et l'urbanisme deviennent des outils importants pour garder les gens en sécurité et en bonne santé face au réchauffement climatique.

**Impact** – Cette idée est importante car elle montre que refroidir les villes ne passe pas seulement par les climatiseurs. Modifier la construction des bâtiments et la planification urbaine peut aider à réduire naturellement la chaleur. Par exemple, planter plus d'arbres, utiliser des matériaux qui réfléchissent la lumière du soleil et concevoir des rues pour laisser passer l'air peuvent rafraîchir les zones urbaines et diminuer les risques pour la santé.

**Prochaine étape** – Les experts et les urbanistes vont probablement travailler davantage à créer des espaces urbains plus frais grâce à des designs et matériaux intelligents. Cette approche pourrait devenir courante pour construire de nouveaux quartiers ou améliorer les quartiers existants afin de protéger les habitants des futures vagues de chaleur.

**Résumé en une phrase** – Concevoir les villes et les bâtiments avec soin devient un moyen essentiel de protéger les populations de la chaleur dangereuse alors que le climat devient plus extrême.
articles.personality_title_es: Cómo el diseño de ciudades ayuda a proteger a las personas de olas de calor mortales
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Europa enfrentó recientemente una ola de calor récord que causó más de 1,300 muertes adicionales, según la Organización Mundial de la Salud (OMS). El calor puede ser peligroso, especialmente en las ciudades donde los edificios y las calles retienen el calor.

**Qué pasó** – Ronita Bardhan, profesora de la Universidad de Cambridge, explicó en una entrevista que la forma en que se diseñan las ciudades y los edificios es clave para proteger a las personas de las olas de calor. Dijo que la arquitectura y el urbanismo se están convirtiendo en herramientas importantes para mantener a las personas seguras y saludables en un mundo que se está calentando.

**Impacto** – Esta idea es importante porque muestra que enfriar las ciudades no es solo usar aires acondicionados. Cambiar cómo se construyen los edificios y planifican las ciudades puede ayudar a reducir el calor de forma natural. Por ejemplo, plantar más árboles, usar materiales que reflejen la luz del sol y diseñar calles que permitan que el aire circule pueden enfriar las áreas urbanas y bajar los riesgos para la salud.

**Próximo paso** – Los expertos y planificadores urbanos probablemente trabajarán más para crear espacios urbanos más frescos usando diseños y materiales inteligentes. Este enfoque podría volverse común para construir nuevos vecindarios o mejorar los existentes y así proteger a las personas de futuras olas de calor.

**Resumen en una frase** – Diseñar ciudades y edificios con cuidado se está volviendo una forma clave de proteger a las personas del calor peligroso a medida que el clima se vuelve más extremo.
articles.image_prompt: A detailed, warm-toned painting of a stylized cityscape where buildings incorporate flowing, organic shapes resembling protective shields and cooling water channels weaving through streets like lifelines, symbolizing architecture and urban design as a vital defense against heat, with soft natural hues of terracotta, muted blues, and gentle greens creating a harmonious, hopeful atmosphere.
